title: Apple’s Hide My Email Service Fails to Hide Your Email
gist: This is a security roundup digest; the captured text does not cover the titled Apple Hide My Email story (that sits past the paywall) but instead two other items: a European Parliament PEGA-committee member being targeted by Pegasus spyware, and senior Google security staff warning that proposed EU pro-competition rules could make Search and Android more vulnerable to hacking.

themes.theme: Pegasus targeting of EU spyware overseers

insights.claim: A member of the European Parliament's PEGA Committee—the body created specifically to investigate spyware abuses including Pegasus—was themselves targeted with Pegasus, per new research released this week.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Confirms commercial spyware is still being deployed against EU lawmakers even as they legislate against it, keeping regulatory and litigation risk elevated for the mercenary-spyware sector and its enablers.
insights.quote: A politician on the European Parliament’s PEGA Committee—created to investigate spyware abuses, including of the notorious Pegasus malware—was targeted with Pegasus himself
insights.locator: A politician on the European Parliament’s PEGA Committee

themes.theme: Google's warning on EU pro-competition rules

insights.claim: Senior Google security staff warned this week that the EU's proposed pro-competition rules could make Google Search and Android systems more vulnerable to hacking and other abuse.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: Signals Google is framing security risk as a lever against EU DMA-style interoperability mandates; watch for this argument in regulatory fights that affect how open Android and Search must become.
insights.quote: top Google security staff warned this week that the pro-competition rule proposals in the EU could make Google Search and Android systems vulnerable to hacking and other abuse
insights.locator: Meanwhile, top Google security staff warned

insights.claim: A regulator's mandated openness is being publicly cast by the regulated incumbent as a direct security tradeoff—more competition framed as more attack surface.
insights.speaker: Matt Burgess
insights.so_what: This security-vs-competition framing is a recurring playbook (Apple used similar arguments on sideloading); investors should expect it to slow or dilute EU interoperability enforcement.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, top Google security staff warned
